1582	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1583	left-whiskering}
1584	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1585	
1586	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1587	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1588	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1589	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1590	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1591	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1592	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1593	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1594	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1595	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1596	
1597	\begin{lemma}
1598	
1599	\leanok
1600	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1601	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1602	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1603	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1604	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1605	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1606	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1607	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1608	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1609	  % "\begin{lemma}
1610	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1611	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1612	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1613	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1614	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1615	  % $$
1616	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1617	  % =
1618	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1619	  % $$
1620	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1621	  % \end{lemma}"
1622	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1623	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1624	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1625	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1626	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1627	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1628	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1629	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1630	  \[
1631	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1632	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1633	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1634	  \]
1635	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1636	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1637	\end{lemma}
1638	
1639	\begin{proof}
1640	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1641	  \leanok
1642	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1643	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1644	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1645	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1646	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1647	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1648	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1649	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1650	  inversion.
1651	
1652	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1653	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1654	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1655	  \[
1656	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1657	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1658	  \]
1659	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1660	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1661	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1662	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1663	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1664	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1665	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1666	  \[
1667	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1668	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1669	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1670	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1671	  \]
1672	
1673	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1674	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1675	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1676	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1677	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1678	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1679	  forward additive comparison map
1680	  \[
1681	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1682	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1683	  \]
1684	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1685	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1686	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1687	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1688	  isomorphism (\texttt{stalk\_tensor\_desc\_forward}).
1689	
1690	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1691	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1692	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1693	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1694	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1695	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1696	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1697	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1698	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1699	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1700	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1701	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1702	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1703	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1704	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1705	
1706	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1707	  \[
1708	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1709	  \]
1710	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1711	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1712	  \[
1713	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1714	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1715	  \]
1716	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1717	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1718	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1719	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1720	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1721	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1722	  neighbourhood system.
1723	
1724	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
1725	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
1726	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
1727	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
1728	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
1729	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
1730	  \[
1731	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
1732	      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
1733	  \]
1734	  and symmetrically on the right variable, where the scalar \(\mathrm{germ}_x r \in R_x\)
1735	  never leaves the stalk ring. This holds because of the germ--scalar compatibility
1736	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot s) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot(\mathrm{germ}_x s)\) of the germ
1737	  map \(R(W) \to R_x\) (the Lean lemma \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\)) — the same germ--scalar
1738	  compatibility already used in stages (i) and (iii). Multiplying a representative
1739	  \(r \cdot a\) and then taking germs produces \((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\),
1740	  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
1741	  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
1742	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
1743	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.
1744	
1745	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
1746	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and
1747	  the balancing read off inside the section module \(A(W) \otimes_{R(W)} B(W)\), then the
1748	  scalar action appearing there is the one produced by the presheaf-of-modules structure
1749	  of \(A\) via restriction of scalars: it lives over the \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier
1750	  \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(W)\), \emph{not} over the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier
1751	  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
1752	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
1753	  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
1754	  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
1755	  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
1756	  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.
1757	
1758	  Where a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier identification is
1759	  genuinely unavoidable, it is the \emph{same} canonical bridge already invoked for the
1760	  stage-(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging (the \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU\_smul}\)/%
1761	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\) step, \texttt{stalk\_tensor\_linear\_map}): the two
1762	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
1763	  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge resolves the obstruction here. Thus the stage-(iv)
1764	  balancing reuses the technique already resolved in stage (iii) rather than re-deriving
1765	  it.
1766	
1767	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
1768	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
1769	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
1770	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
1771	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
1772	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
1773	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
1774	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
1775	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
1776	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
1777	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
1778	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
1779	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
1780	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
1781	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
1782	
1783	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
1784	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
1785	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
1786	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
1787	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
1788	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
1789	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
1790	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
1791	  inverse.
1792	\end{proof}
1793	
1794	The proof of the live whiskering lemma below factors through two named
1795	sub-lemmas: the \emph{d.1-bridge} relating membership in the sheafification
1796	localizer \(J.W\) to stalkwise isomorphism, and the \emph{second-factor
1797	naturality} of the d.2 commutation isomorphism \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}.
1798	We state both before the lemma that consumes them.
1799	
1800	\begin{lemma}
1801	
1802	\leanok
1803	[Unconditional left-whiskering of the localizer, via d.2]
1804	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1805	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
1806	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1807	  Let \(R\), \(J\) be as above with \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\),
1808	  let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\)
1809	  be a morphism whose underlying morphism lies in \(J.W\). Then the left-whiskered
1810	  morphism \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\) is \(J\)-locally injective
1811	  (indeed lies in \(J.W\)). No flatness and no local-triviality hypothesis on \(F\) is
1812	  required. This discharges the open left-whiskering obligation
1813	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} that the unconditional associator
1814	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes.
1815	\end{lemma}
1816	
1817	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (Archon-original assembly from the Stacks stalk-tensor lemma
1818	% above and the stalkwise criterion for membership in the sheafification localizer;
1819	% no single external source proof.)
1820	\begin{proof}
1821	  \leanok
1822	  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1823	  The proof runs in three movements: the d.1-bridge turns membership in \(J.W\) into a
1824	  stalkwise statement, the second-factor naturality of the d.2 isomorphism transports
1825	  the whiskered stalk into a tensor of the stalk maps, and a flatness-free
1826	  ``tensoring by an equivalence'' argument concludes.
1827	
1828	  \emph{(a) The d.1-bridge.} Specialise to the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
1829	  where stalks exist. By the hypothesis \(g \in J.W\) and the d.1-bridge
1830	  \texttt{W\_implies\_stalkwise\_iso}, the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map \(g_x : M_x \to N_x\)
1831	  is an isomorphism at every point \(x \in X\). By \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} this
1832	  stalk map carries an \(R_x\)-linear structure, and the bijectivity just obtained
1833	  upgrades it to an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
1834	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso} : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\).
1835	
1836	  \emph{(b) Identifying the whiskered stalk.} By the second-factor naturality of the
1837	  stalk--tensor comparison \texttt{stalk\_tensor\_commutation\_naturality\_right}, the
1838	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1839	  identifies the stalk \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) of the whiskered morphism with the
1840	  map
1841	  \[
1842	    \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x \;=\; \mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x
1843	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \longrightarrow F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1844	  \]
1845	
1846	  \emph{(c) Conclusion.} Tensoring the \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
1847	  \(g_x : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) of (a) on the left by the identity of \(F_x\)
1848	  yields an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
1849	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\)
1850	  (this is \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.lTensor}\); it needs \emph{no} flatness of \(F_x\),
1851	  because lifting an isomorphism through \(F_x \otimes_{R_x} (-)\) only uses the
1852	  functoriality of the tensor product, and the inverse is supplied by the inverse
1853	  equivalence). By (b), \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is therefore an isomorphism of
1854	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalks for every \(x\). Applying the converse half of the d.1-bridge
1855	  \texttt{W\_implies\_stalkwise\_iso} to the morphism \(F \triangleleft g\) gives
1856	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\), and in particular it is \(J\)-locally injective
1857	  (\(\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective}\)). No flatness, no local
1858	  triviality, and the whiskering object \(F\) is arbitrary.
1859	
1860	  The statement holds over any Grothendieck site \(J\) satisfying
1861	  \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\), and is applied in particular
1862	  at the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) of a scheme, where the stalk
1863	  arguments of (a)--(c) are available and through which the unconditional associator
1864	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized.
1865	\end{proof}

1876	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1877	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1878	
1879	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1880	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1881	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1882	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1883	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1884	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1885	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1886	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1887	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1888	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1889	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1890	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1891	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1892	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1934	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible

1940	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1941	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1942	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1943	  \[
1944	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
1945	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1946	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1947	  \]
1948	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
1949	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
1950	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
1951	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
1952	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
1953	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
1954	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1955	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1956	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1957	  \(\otimes_X\).
1958	\end{definition}

2015	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2016	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
2017	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
2018	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
2019	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
2020	  01CR).
2021	
2022	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
2023	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
2024	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
2025	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
2026	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2027	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
2028	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
2029	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2030	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
2031	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
2032	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
2033	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
2034	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
2035	
2036	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
2037	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
2038	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
2039	  \[
2040	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
2041	    \quad
2042	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
2043	    \quad
2044	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
2045	    \quad
2046	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
2047	  \]
2048	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
2049	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2050	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
2051	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
2052	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
2053	  any of the four axiom proofs.
2054	
2055	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
2056	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
2057	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
2058	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
2059	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
2060	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
2061	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
2062	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
2063	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
2064	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
2065	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
2066	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
2067	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
2068	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
2069	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
2070	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
2071	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
2072	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
2073	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
2074	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
2075	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
2076	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
2077	  pinned commit.
2078

2109	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the

2157	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2158	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2159	
2160	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) carries its line
2161	bundles on the \emph{local-triviality} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)
2162	(\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}): its carrier \(\mathtt{OnProduct}\) is the
2163	subobject \(\{\,L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,(C \times_S T) \mid
2164	\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\,\}\) of locally-trivial \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, and
2165	its functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules:
2166	the projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the
2167	base-change pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to
2168	descend to the carrier and to be \emph{additive} (an \(\mathtt{AddMonoidHom}\) of
2169	isomorphism classes) one needs the comparison isomorphism
2170	\[
2171	  f^*(L \otimes_X L')
2172	    \;\cong\;
2173	  f^*L \otimes_Y f^*L'
2174	\]
2175	for \emph{locally-trivial} \(L, L'\) and a \emph{general} morphism of schemes
2176	\(f : Y \to X\) --- the projection and the base-change morphism are neither open
2177	immersions nor flat. This restricted comparison isomorphism, on locally-trivial
2178	pairs, is the load-bearing result of the section
2179	(\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}); the invertibility corollary
2180	\texttt{isinvertible\_pullback} is then a three-line consequence. The comparison
2181	is needed \emph{only} on locally-trivial pairs --- the carrier never presents a
2182	general \(\mathcal{O}\)-module --- so no comparison for arbitrary modules is
2183	required.
2184	
2185	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2186	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2187	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2188	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2189	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2190	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(\mathtt{appIso}\,f\)). A general scheme
2191	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2192	
2193	\paragraph{The reduction to two facts about pullback.} The corollary
2194	\texttt{isinvertible\_pullback} is a three-line consequence of two facts about
2195	pullback, exactly as in the Stacks proof of \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}. The
2196	first is that pullback commutes with the tensor product on the \emph{locally-trivial}
2197	pairs the consumer presents: the canonical comparison
2198	\(f^*(L \otimes_X L') \cong f^*L \otimes_Y f^*L'\) is an isomorphism whenever
2199	\(L, L'\) are locally trivial, for every \(f\)
2200	(\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}). The second is that pullback preserves the
2201	structure sheaf, \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\)
2202	(\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}, already in hand). Given a locally-trivial inverse
2203	\(N\) with \(L \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), pulling back yields
2204	\[
2205	  f^*L \otimes_Y f^*N
2206	    \;\cong\; f^*(L \otimes_X N)
2207	    \;\cong\; f^*\mathcal{O}_X
2208	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
2209	\]
2210	so \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of \(f^*L\). The locally-trivial comparison
2211	\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} is the load-bearing input.
2212	
2213	\paragraph{Why the apparent obstruction does not apply.} It is tempting to argue that
2214	the comparison can never be an isomorphism because an (op)lax monoidal functor need
2215	not preserve invertible objects --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal, yet
2216	\(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible. But that
2217	counterexample concerns the \emph{lax} tensorator of \emph{pushforward}: \(\Gamma\)
2218	is a right adjoint (global sections), and its comparison
2219	\(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes
2220	\mathcal{G})\) genuinely fails to be an isomorphism. The comparison we need is the
2221	\emph{oplax} \(\delta\) of \emph{pullback}, a \emph{left} adjoint, running the other
2222	way, \(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\). Pullback \emph{provably preserves
2223	local triviality} (\cref{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}): if \(M\) is trivialised
2224	by \(\mathcal{O}_U\) on a cover \(\{U_i\}\), then \(f^*M\) is trivialised by
2225	\(\mathcal{O}_{f^{-1}U_i}\) on \(\{f^{-1}U_i\}\) --- using only
2226	\(f^*\mathcal{O} \cong \mathcal{O}\) and the compatibility of pullback with
2227	restriction along open immersions. Consequently, on locally-trivial objects, both
2228	sides of \(\delta\) are locally trivial, and \(\delta\) restricts to a comparison
2229	between the structure sheaf and itself on each chart, where it is forced to be an
2230	isomorphism. The bare oplax structure supplies \(\delta\) as a \emph{morphism} for
2231	free (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}); the entire remaining content is the
2232	chart-local check that, on a trivialising cover, \(\delta\) is an isomorphism.
2233	
2234	\paragraph{The construction route.} The isomorphism is obtained by \emph{upgrading
2235	the already-built oplax comparison map} \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} =
2236	\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) to an isomorphism
2237	through a chart-chase --- the same shape as the proven local-triviality results
2238	\cref{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback} and
2239	\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, and \emph{not} via any concrete
2240	inverse-image model or filtered-colimit construction. The argument decomposes into
2241	four steps, carried out in the sub-lemmas cited by
2242	\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}:
2243	\begin{itemize}
2244	  \item[(D1$'$)] \emph{Naturality of \(\delta\) in \((M,N)\).} The sheaf-level
2245	    comparison \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is natural in both arguments, assembled from
2246	    the naturality of the abstract oplax \(\delta\) (Mathlib's
2247	    \(\mathtt{Functor.OplaxMonoidal.}\delta\mathtt{\_natural}\)) and of the
2248	    sheafification reconciliations used to build it
2249	    (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural}). This naturality is what lets a chart
2250	    replace \(M|_{U_i}, N|_{U_i}\) by the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}\) without
2251	    changing whether \(\delta\) is an isomorphism there.
2252	  \item[(D2$'$)] \emph{\(\delta\) on the unit pair \((\mathcal{O},\mathcal{O})\) is an
2253	    isomorphism.} On the trivial bundle the comparison
2254	    \(\delta_{(\mathcal{O},\mathcal{O})} : f^*(\mathcal{O} \otimes \mathcal{O}) \to
2255	    f^*\mathcal{O} \otimes f^*\mathcal{O}\) is reduced, by the \(\delta\)-wrapping
2256	    \(\mathtt{isIso\_sheafifyDelta\_unitPair\_of\_isIso\_sheafifyEta}\) (the presheaf
2257	    oplax left-unitality factored through sheafification, using
2258	    \(\mathtt{W\_of\_isIso\_sheafification}\) and the flatness-free right-whiskering
2259	    \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)), to the single \emph{unit square}
2260	    \(a_Y.\mathrm{map}(\eta\,F) \circ \mathtt{sheafifyUnitIso} = \mathtt{pullbackValIso}
2261	    \circ \mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) (\texttt{eta\_bridge\_unit\_square}). That
2262	    square, transposed across the sheaf pullback--pushforward adjunction, bottoms out in
2263	    the structure-sheaf comparison \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\)
2264	    (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}, an isomorphism for \emph{all} \(f\)); hence
2265	    \(\delta_{(\mathcal{O},\mathcal{O})}\) is an isomorphism
2266	    (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_unit}).
2267	  \item[(D3$'$)] \emph{Composition coherence of \(\delta\)} (the genuinely new
2268	    ingredient). For composable scheme morphisms \(h, f\) the comparison \(\delta\) for
2269	    the composite \(h \circ f\) factors through the comparisons for \(f\) and for \(h\),
2270	    conjugated by the pullback pseudofunctoriality isomorphism
2271	    \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\) (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_basechange}).
2272	    Specialising to \(h := j_i'\) at an open \(U_i\) of \(X\) with preimage
2273	    \(f^{-1}U_i\) --- with \(g_i : f^{-1}U_i \to U_i\) the restriction of \(f\) and
2274	    \(j_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), \(j_i' : f^{-1}U_i \hookrightarrow Y\) the open
2275	    immersions, so \(f \circ j_i' = j_i \circ g_i\) --- this intertwines the
2276	    restriction-to-\(f^{-1}U_i\) of \(\delta\) for \(f\) with the \(\delta\) for
2277	    \(g_i\). Because \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) is a hand-built four-fold composite
2278	    rather than an adjunction transpose, the unit-coherence mate calculus of
2279	    \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} does \emph{not} transfer; the coherence is
2280	    instead established as a paste of four composition-coherence squares, decomposing
2281	    \(\delta\) of the composite pullback via Mathlib's
2282	    \(\mathtt{Functor.OplaxMonoidal.comp\_}\delta\) and the conjugation
2283	    \(\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}\) of the pseudofunctor isomorphisms.
2284	  \item[(D4$'$)] \emph{Chart-chase assembly.} Choose a common affine cover \(\{U_i\}\)
2285	    of \(X\) trivialising both \(M\) and \(N\) (the common refinement, exactly as in
2286	    \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). On each \(f^{-1}U_i\), the
2287	    base-change coherence (D3$'$) aligns the restriction of \(\delta\) with the
2288	    \(\delta\) for \(g_i\); naturality (D1$'$) replaces the trivialised
2289	    \(M|_{U_i}, N|_{U_i}\) by \(\mathcal{O}\); and (D2$'$) makes the resulting
2290	    comparison on the unit pair an isomorphism. Thus \(\delta\) restricts to an
2291	    isomorphism on every member of the cover \(\{f^{-1}U_i\}\) of \(Y\), and the
2292	    restriction-detects-isomorphism criterion
2293	    \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict} promotes it to a global isomorphism. The
2294	    structure mirrors the chase of \cref{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}.
2295	\end{itemize}
2296	The only genuinely new sub-step is (D3$'$); (D1$'$) is Mathlib naturality, (D2$'$)
2297	is the unit coherence backed by \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\), and (D4$'$) reuses the
2298	already-proven cover machinery
2299	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial},
2300	\cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}). No concrete inverse-image model, no
2301	filtered-colimit/\(\otimes\) interchange, and no general comparison for arbitrary
2302	modules is constructed.

2304	\begin{lemma}
2305	
2306	\leanok
2307	[The presheaf pushforward is lax monoidal]
2308	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_laxmonoidal}
2309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal}
2310	  Let \(\varphi\) be a morphism of \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued ring presheaves,
2311	  presenting a pushforward of presheaves of modules. Then the presheaf pushforward
2312	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\) is \emph{lax monoidal}: it
2313	  carries the comparison
2314	  \[
2315	    \mu :
2316	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
2317	        \otimes (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
2318	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2319	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B),
2320	  \]
2321	  obtained as the composite of the strong-monoidal pushforward of presheaves over a
2322	  fixed ring presheaf (\(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\)) with the lax
2323	  monoidal restriction of scalars \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) along the
2324	  ring-presheaf map. This is a statement at the \emph{presheaf} level of
2325	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules; it does not require a monoidal structure on
2326	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\).
2327	\end{lemma}
2328	
2329	\begin{proof}
2330	
2331	  Restriction of scalars along a homomorphism of ring presheaves is lax monoidal --- the
2332	  comparison sends \((a \otimes b)\) to itself under the identification of the
2333	  restricted tensor product with the source tensor product --- and the pushforward of
2334	  presheaves of modules over a \emph{fixed} ring presheaf is strong monoidal. The
2335	  presheaf pushforward along \(\varphi\) factors as the composite of these two functors,
2336	  so its lax monoidal structure is the composite of the strong-monoidal structure on the
2337	  first factor with the lax structure on the second; the comparison \(\mu\) is the
2338	  whiskered composite of the two comparisons.
2339	\end{proof}

2613	
2614	\begin{proof}
2615	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:pullback_obj_unit_to_unit_iso}
2616	  This is a one-step consequence of a Mathlib instance, with no chart-chase. The
2617	  canonical comparison map is the genuine Mathlib morphism
2618	  \[
2619	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,f :
2620	      f^*\mathcal{O}_X \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
2621	  \]
2622	  the unit comparison \(\eta\) of \(f^*\); abbreviate it \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\). Mathlib
2623	  proves this map is an isomorphism whenever the comparison functor
2624	  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}\) on opens is \(\mathtt{Final}\): the instance
2625	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\).
2626	
2627	  The point is that the finality hypothesis holds \emph{unconditionally}, for every
2628	  morphism of schemes \(f\). The preimage functor \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}
2629	  : \mathtt{Opens}\,X \to \mathtt{Opens}\,Y\) is a frame homomorphism: it preserves
2630	  arbitrary unions and finite intersections, hence preserves all finite limits. A
2631	  functor between small categories that preserves finite limits is representably flat,
2632	  so \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\) gives \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathtt{Final}\)
2633	  with no hypothesis on \(f\) whatsoever. (There is no need to pass to affine charts:
2634	  finality is not a special feature of open immersions but a general property of
2635	  preimage-on-opens.)

2638	  directly, and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The asserted

2672	  The map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) is built (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) by
2673	  transporting the abstract oplax comparison \(\delta\) of the presheaf pullback
2674	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}) through the sheafification
2675	  reconciliations. The asserted square is the pasting of four naturality squares, one for
2676	  each ingredient of this composite; we record them in order, the only delicate one being the
2677	  naturality of the oplax comparison itself.
2678	
2679	  \emph{Square 2: naturality of the oplax comparison \(\delta\).} The comparison \(\delta\) is
2680	  the oplax-monoidal comparison of the presheaf-level pullback functor
2681	  \(F = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi'\), whose defining ring map
2682	  \(\varphi' = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\) has, as the source of its domain,
2683	  the ring presheaf of \(X\). For the monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) to
2684	  be available on this domain the ring presheaf must be presented in its \emph{canonical}
2685	  form as the composite

2687	  presentation on which that monoidal structure is defined. When the comparison map's
2688	  construction is unfolded, however, the domain ring re-presents in a form definitionally
2689	  equal, but not explicitly identical, to the canonical one, and on that form the
2690	  monoidal structure is not directly available. The naturality of \(\delta\),
2691	  \[
2692	    \delta \circ F(a \otimes b) \;=\; (Fa \otimes Fb) \circ \delta,
2693	  \]
2694	  which holds for any oplax monoidal functor, is therefore applied by re-establishing the
2695	  canonical domain-ring spelling at the functor argument itself: one ascribes the defining
2696	  ring map \(\varphi'\) explicitly to the canonical composite-functor source, so that the
2697	  domain monoidal structure resolves, and the naturality identity then matches the goal
2698	  definitionally. This is a structural device --- it changes only the presentation of
2699	  \(F\)'s defining ring map. No restatement of \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) or of its helper
2700	  isomorphisms is needed, and the unit-case lemma \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap\_unit\_isIso}\)
2701	  (D2\(^\prime\)) is unaffected. After this step the comparison \(\delta\) commutes past

2726	  module arguments. Equivalently: after descending to sections over each open \(U\) and
2727	  passing to the underlying module components, the naturality square is the

2731	
2732	\begin{proof}
2733	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_map}
2734	  Write \(\Phi\) for the natural transformation whose naturality square (in the two module
2735	  arguments \(M, N\), along morphisms \(a : M \to M'\) and \(b : N \to N'\)) is to be shown
2736	  to commute; \(\Phi\) is assembled from the reconciliation isomorphism
2737	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) (the intermediate construction inside
2738	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}), so its components are morphisms of presheaves of modules.
2739	  The square is closed at the categorical (tensor-of-morphisms) level, without any descent to
2740	  sections or to elements.
2741	
2742	  \emph{Step 1: a single tensor-of-morphisms presentation.} The \(\mathrm{hom}\) leg of
2743	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) is rewritten as a \emph{single} tensor of morphisms of the
2744	  form \(a.\mathrm{map}(\eta_P \otimes \eta_Q)\), where
2745	  \(\eta = (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}).\mathrm{unit}\) is the unit
2746	  \(\mathbf{1} \Rightarrow (\text{sheafify}) \circ (\text{inclusion})\) of the sheafification
2747	  adjunction and \(a\) is the sheafification functor. The essential point is that all factors
2748	  are kept inside \emph{one} monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) --- the
2749	  canonical presentation as a composite with the forgetful functor \(\mathtt{forget}_2\) ---
2750	  so that the bifunctoriality law of \(\otimes\) can be applied consistently within one
2751	  monoidal structure.
2752	
2753	  \emph{Step 2: merge the two functor-image factors.} Both composites around the square are
2754	  images, under the sheafification functor, of a tensor of morphisms. The two functor-image
2755	  factors are merged into a single image of a composite by functoriality,
2756	  \(F(g) \circ F(h) = F(g \circ h)\).
2757	
2758	  \emph{Step 3: bifunctoriality and unit naturality.} The resulting equality of two tensors of
2759	  morphisms is closed by the bifunctoriality (interchange) law
2760	  \[
2761	    (f_1 \otimes f_2) \circ (g_1 \otimes g_2) \;=\; (f_1 \circ g_1) \otimes (f_2 \circ g_2),
2762	  \]
2763	  applied within the single chosen monoidal structure, together with the two
2764	  single-component naturality squares of the sheafification unit \(\eta\), one in each module

2772	  single-component squares through the interchange law equates the two tensors of morphisms,
2773	  which is exactly the naturality square of \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\).

2842	\subsection{The unit square (D2$'$): a mate-calculus telescope}
2843	
2844	The proof of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_unit} bottoms out in the single morphism equation
2845	labelled the \emph{unit square} below. Its proof is a telescope across three nested adjunction
2846	layers (sheafification, presheaf pullback--pushforward, sheaf pullback--pushforward). This
2847	subsection isolates the load-bearing intermediate identities as named lemmas so each can be
2848	discharged independently, then assembles them.
2849	
2850	We fix throughout a morphism of schemes \(f : Y \to X\) and write
2851	\(\varphi = f.\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\), \(\varphi' = \varphi.\mathrm{hom}\). Let \(a_X, a_Y\)
2852	denote sheafification of presheaves of modules on \(X\), \(Y\); \(\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\) the
2853	presheaf monoidal unit; \(F = \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'\) the presheaf pullback;
2854	\(\eta\,F : F.\mathrm{obj}\,\mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}} \to \mathbf{1}^{\mathrm{p}}\) its oplax unit
2855	comparison; and \(\varepsilon(G)\) the lax unit comparison of a lax monoidal functor \(G\). The
2856	isomorphisms \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,f\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and \(\mathtt{sheafifyUnitIso}\) are
2857	the file's comparison morphisms relating the sheaf-level pullback value to the sheafification of
2858	the presheaf-level pullback. Write
2859	\(\mathtt{sheafCounit}_\bullet = (\mathtt{sheafificationAdjunction}\,(\mathbf{1}_{\bullet.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{val}})).\mathrm{counit}\).

2867	  adjunctions and \(A = \mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{comp}\,\mathtt{adj}_2 : L_1 \circ L_2 \dashv
2868	  R_2 \circ R_1\) their composite. Then the hom-set bijection of the composite factors as the
2869	  composite of the two factor bijections: for every \(g\),

2873	    \mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{homEquiv}\bigl(\mathtt{adj}_2.\mathrm{homEquiv}\,g\bigr).

2876	  \(a_X \circ \mathtt{pullback}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\,\varphi\)), the head of step~2 of
2877	  \texttt{eta\_bridge\_unit\_square} equals

2885	  pasting \(\mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{unit} \mathbin{;} R_1(\mathtt{adj}_2.\mathrm{unit})_{L_1(-)}\)
2886	  and its counit dually; equivalently its hom-set bijection \(A.\mathrm{homEquiv}\) is the
2887	  composite of the two factor bijections in the order \(\mathtt{adj}_2.\mathrm{homEquiv}\) then
2888	  \(\mathtt{adj}_1.\mathrm{homEquiv}\). This is the standard factorisation
2889	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.comp\_homEquiv}\) (the naturality of \(\mathtt{homEquiv}\) under
2890	  composition of adjunctions): a morphism \(L_1 L_2\,c \to d\) is transposed first across

3144	  pivot is that, for the canonical oplax structure on a left adjoint
3145	  (\(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\), here
3146	  \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}), \(\delta\) is \emph{itself} an adjunction
3147	  transpose: \(\delta_{M,N} = \mathtt{homEquiv}^{-1}\bigl((\eta_{M}\otimes\eta_{N})
3148	  \mathbin{;} \mu_{M,N}\bigr)\), where \(\mu\) is the lax tensorator of the
3149	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\). Consequently the transpose-and-inject opening that closes the
3150	  unit coherence ports verbatim: applying \(\mathtt{homEquiv}.\mathrm{injective}\) for the
3151	  pullback--pushforward adjunction and transporting \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) to
3152	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) across the adjunctions via
3153	  \(\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}\) (with \(\mathtt{unit\_conjugateEquiv}\)
3154	  and \(\mathtt{Adjunction.comp\_unit\_app}\)) reduces the goal to the lax-\(\mu\)
3155	  composition coherence of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pushforward}\) across
3156	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\). This last residual --- isolated as the lemma
3157	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp\_lax\_}\mu\) --- is the \emph{right-adjoint twin} of the
3158	  \(\delta\)-coherence: it is precisely the assertion that the pseudofunctoriality
3159	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) is \emph{monoidal} for the lax tensorators
3160	  \(\mu\), i.e.\ that comparing the lax-\(\mu\) structure of the single composite
3161	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\) with the composite of the two individual lax-\(\mu\) structures
3162	  agrees across \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\). Two points must be stated precisely.
3163	
3164	  First, the mate-calculus reduction proceeds as follows: the transpose-and-inject
3165	  opening, the \(\delta = \mathtt{homEquiv}^{-1}\bigl((\eta\otimes\eta)\mathbin{;}\mu\bigr)\)
3166	  pivot, the \(\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}\) transport across the adjunctions
3167	  (with \(\mathtt{unit\_conjugateEquiv}\) and \(\mathtt{Adjunction.comp\_unit\_app}\)), and
3168	  the two-argument \(\mathtt{tensorHom}\)/\(\delta_{\mathrm{natural}}\) bookkeeping --- which
3169	  follows the template of Mathlib's
3170	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.isMonoidal\_comp}\) (the canonical ``a composite of
3171	  monoidal adjunctions is monoidal'') --- together reduce Sq2b to the single residual
3172	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp\_lax\_}\mu\). This is the content of
3173	  \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}'s \(\delta\)-mirror.
3174	
3175	  Second, this residual \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp\_lax\_}\mu\) is discharged by a short
3176	  \emph{sectionwise pure-tensor collapse}, not by a change-of-rings calculation. On the
3177	  strongly-monoidal pushforward objects the pushforward tensorator \(\mu\) reduces
3178	  \emph{definitionally} to the lighter \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) tensorator \(\mu\) (the
3179	  identity \(\mathtt{pushforward\_}\mu\mathtt{\_eq}\), which holds by reflexivity); after
3180	  this reduction, evaluated on a pure tensor \(m \otimes n\) every
3181	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) tensorator is the identity (Mathlib's
3182	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars\_}\mu\mathtt{\_tmul}\)), so both sides of the
3183	  interchange collapse to the same pure tensor \(m \otimes n\); no change-of-rings
3184	  construction is required. This closes the Sq2b obligation.
3185	
3186	  Crucially, this sub-step is stated at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level --- about
3187	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'\) with \(\varphi'\) the ring map
3188	  \((\mathcal{S}_{0}\mathbin{;}\mathtt{forget}_{2}) \to f^{\mathrm{op}}\mathbin{;}
3189	  (\mathcal{R}_{0}\mathbin{;}\mathtt{forget}_{2})\) carried by \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\)
3190	  --- rather than at the scheme level, where the composite factorisation is immediate from
3191	  \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) and the ring-map reconciliation is definitional.
3192	
3193	  The observation above --- that \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) is not an adjunction
3194	  transpose --- concerns the \emph{full} four-fold composite and does not constrain
3195	  Sq2b: the inner tensorator \(\delta\) genuinely \emph{is} a transpose
3196	  (\(\mathtt{homEquiv}^{-1}\) of \((\eta\otimes\eta)\mathbin{;}\mu\)), so for Sq2b
3197	  specifically the \(\eta\to\delta\) mirror of \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} is the
3198	  correct route.
3199	
3200	  \emph{Sq1 (sheafification \(\leftrightarrow\) pullback) --- the sole open ingredient.} The
3201	  composition coherence of \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) across
3202	  \(h \circ f\) --- the analog of \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) for the
3203	  ``sheafify-then-pullback'' natural isomorphism --- is \emph{absent from Mathlib} and is
3204	  supplied as a named, private project sub-lemma
3205	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp}\). For a presheaf \(P\) over \(X\), writing
3206	  \(a_X, a_Z\) for the sheafifications and \(\mathtt{sheafCompPb} =
3207	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\), it asserts
3208	  \[
3209	    ((\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\,(h \circ f)).\mathrm{app}\,P).\mathrm{hom}
3210	      \;=\;
3211	      (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}_{a_X P} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3212	      h^{*}\bigl(((\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\,f).\mathrm{app}\,P).\mathrm{hom}\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
3213	      ((\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\,h).\mathrm{app}\,
3214	        (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f}\,P)).\mathrm{hom} \;\mathbin{;}\;
3215	      a_Z.\mathrm{map}\bigl(
3216	        (\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp}\,\varphi'_{f}\,\varphi'_{h}).\mathrm{hom}_P
3217	      \bigr).
3218	  \]
3219	  It is proved by the \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) mate calculus: both \(\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\)
3220	  isomorphisms are \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) of composite adjunctions, so transposing the
3221	  identity under the composite adjunction for \(h \circ f\) and evaluating the left-hand side
3222	  by the mate identity \(\mathtt{homEquiv\_leftAdjointUniq\_hom\_app}\) reduces it to a
3223	  unit-level identity; the two \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) factors are then transported across
3224	  the adjunction units --- the \(\delta\)-free twin of
3225	  \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
3226	
3227	  \emph{The reduced tail goal.} After peeling the leading pseudofunctoriality factor
3228	  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}\), the remaining obligation is a unit
3229	  identity for the two-layer composite adjunction (presheaf pullback--pushforward adjunction
3230	  composed with the sheafification adjunction), isolated as the named sub-lemma
3231	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp\_tail}\). Writing \(B_{\varphi}\) for the
3232	  sheaf-level composite adjunction of \cref{lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta_general} and
3233	  \(\mathtt{PresheafPullback}_f\) for the presheaf-level pullback along \(f\), the assembly
3234	  proceeds in the following ordered steps, mirroring the already-closed
3235	  \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (whose proof is the \(\delta\)-free analog of this one,
3236	  exactly one sheafification layer down):
3237	  \begin{enumerate}
3238	    \item[(a)] \emph{Strip the outer identity wrapper.} The tail goal carries an outer
3239	      \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}(\mathbf{1})\) change-of-rings wrapper coming from the
3240	      identity ring map; this restriction-of-scalars-along-identity factor is removed first,
3241	      so that the genuine comparison factors are exposed.
3242	    \item[(b)] \emph{Distribute the right-hand sheaf composite under \(\mathtt{forget}\).} The
3243	      right-hand side is the sheaf-level composite carrying the two sub-comparison factors
3244	      \(R_1\) (the \(f\)-layer) and \(R_5\) (the \(h\)-layer). Pushing the forgetful functor
3245	      \(\mathtt{forget}\) (sheaf \(\to\) presheaf) through the composite separates these two
3246	      factors \emph{without} disturbing the left-hand composite unit \(B_{h\circ f}.\mathrm{unit}\),
3247	      which is left intact for the final reassembly.
3248	    \item[(c)] \emph{Recover the sub-comparison units.} Apply
3249	      \cref{lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta_general}
3250	      (\(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniqUnitEta\_app}\), the \(P\)-general step-1 brick) to rewrite
3251	      \(R_1\) as the composite-adjunction unit \(B_f.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,P\) and
3252	      \(R_5\) as \(B_h.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,(\mathtt{PresheafPullback}_f\,P)\). This is
3253	      precisely the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints characterisation
3254	      \(\mathtt{homEquiv\_leftAdjointUniq\_hom\_app}\), now used in its \(P\)-general form.
3255	    \item[(d)] \emph{Slide the \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) comparison past the recovered
3256	      units.} The sheaf-level comparison \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f\) is moved across the
3257	      two recovered units \(B_f.\mathrm{unit}\) and \(B_h.\mathrm{unit}\) by its naturality.
3258	    \item[(e)] \emph{Reassemble the composite unit.} Expanding
3259	      \(B_{h\circ f}.\mathrm{unit}\) by the composite-adjunction unit formula
3260	      \(\mathtt{comp\_unit\_app}\) and applying \(\mathtt{unit\_naturality}\) identifies the
3261	      slid expression of step~(d) with the left-hand composite unit, closing the tail.
3262	  \end{enumerate}
3263	
3264	  \emph{The precise binding obligation.} The one place where the assembly is not yet a
3265	  mechanical paste is the bridge between the \emph{presheaf}-level left-hand data (the
3266	  presheaf pushforward \(\mathtt{pushforward}^{\mathrm{pre}}\) and the composite unit
3267	  \(B_{h\circ f}.\mathrm{unit}\)) and the \emph{sheaf}-level right-hand factors \(R_1, R_5\)
3268	  wrapped in \(\mathtt{forget}\). Crossing this boundary requires the compatibility
3269	  \[
3270	    \mathtt{forget} \circ \mathtt{pushforward}^{\mathrm{sheaf}}
3271	      \;=\;
3272	    \mathtt{pushforward}^{\mathrm{pre}} \circ \mathtt{forget},
3273	  \]
3274	  interacting with the recovered \(B_f\)- and \(B_h\)-units of step~(c). This compatibility is
3275	  the natural unit to isolate as its own named sub-lemma \emph{before} the assembly: once it
3276	  is in hand, steps (a)--(e) become a mechanical paste, whereas without it the presheaf- and
3277	  sheaf-level pushforwards cannot be aligned at the recovered units. The remainder of the tail
3278	  is bookkeeping around this single compatibility.
3279	
3280	  This is the sheafification-laden analog of \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}; unlike
3281	  that lemma it is not definitional sectionwise, because the adjunctions involved are
3282	  composite rather than single transposes. The outer Sq1 lemma
3283	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp}\) \emph{consumes} this tail: it peels the leading
3284	  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}\) factor and then invokes
3285	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp\_tail}\) for the residual unit identity.
3286	  \emph{Warning:} transposing the \emph{whole} tail back under the composite adjunction --- as
3287	  opposed to recovering the two sub-units individually via step~(c) and reassembling --- is
3288	  \emph{circular} (verified): it returns the very identity one set out to prove, so the
3289	  step-(c) recovery of the individual \(R_1, R_5\) units is essential.
3290	
3291	  \emph{Sq3 (unit-iso transport).} The monoidal-unit sheafification isomorphism
3292	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) is carried through the same \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\)
3293	  identification used for Sq2.
3294	
3295	  \emph{Sq4 (the connecting iso).} The scheme-level composition coherence of
3296	  \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) relates \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,(h \circ f)\,M\) to
3297	  \(h^{*}\bigl(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,f\,M\bigr)\),
3298	  \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,h\,(f^{*}M)\), and the pseudofunctoriality component
3299	  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M\); it produces the final
3300	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\bigl((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M,
3301	  (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_N\bigr)\) factor. It is \emph{absent from Mathlib and not yet
3302	  built}; but \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) factors through \(\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\) (as
3303	  \(\mathtt{sheafCompPb}^{-1}\) followed by the pullback of a counit), so its composition
3304	  coherence reduces to that of Sq1 together with the pseudofunctoriality of the counit ---
3305	  i.e.\ Sq4 becomes a short corollary once Sq1 is in hand.
3306	
3307	  This Sq4 coherence is a \emph{standalone named obligation}, to be built separately and not
3308	  folded into Sq1: it is the \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) \emph{composition-coherence} sub-lemma
3309	  --- the naturality across the connecting-object boundary where the abstract pullback's
3310	  \(\mathtt{.val}\) presentation meets the helper-\(\mathtt{.obj}\) presentation --- that is
3311	  consumed inside \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap\_restrict}\). The prover should treat it as its own
3312	  brick (a corollary of Sq1, but with its own statement and its own connecting-object
3313	  bookkeeping), not as a step inlined in the proof of Sq1.
3314	
3315	  The two remaining ingredients are Sq1 (\(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp}\)) and,
3316	  downstream of it, Sq4: the monoidality Sq2b (\(\mathtt{pullbackComp\_}\delta\) together with
3317	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp\_lax\_}\mu\)) follows from the mate-calculus reduction above, the
3318	  Sq3 unit-iso transport follows from the same \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) identification, and
3319	  the Sq2 ring-map reconciliation is definitional. Sq4 reduces to Sq1
3320	  via the \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) factorisation, so Sq1 is the open target.
3321	  The adjunction-mate bridge \(\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}\) (relating
3322	  \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) for the left adjoints to \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) for the
3323	  right adjoints) is the device used in Sq2b and Sq4 to transport the pseudofunctoriality
3324	  coherence across the adjunctions.
3325	
3326	  The four squares do \emph{not} paste row-by-row. Because \(S_1^{h}\) acts on
3327	  \(\bigl((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M\bigr) \otimes
3328	  \bigl((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N\bigr)\), \emph{not} on
3329	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f}\,(M \otimes N)\), the factors must first be slid past one
3330	  another by \(\delta_{\mathrm{natural}}\) and the naturality of the intervening natural
3331	  transformations before assembly, exactly as in the D1$'$ naturality paste of
3332	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural}. With Sq1 in hand, the Sq2 oplax decomposition, the
3333	  Sq3 unit-iso transport, and the Sq4 connecting-iso coherence assemble the four squares into
3334	  the displayed identity. The base-change-square consequence (the Remark) is then the
3335	  specialisation \(h := j'\) obtained by equating the two factorisations \(j' \mathbin{;} f =
3336	  g \mathbin{;} j\); the iff-clause is immediate, an isomorphism being preserved and reflected
3337	  by the pseudofunctoriality isomorphism it is conjugated by.
3338	\end{proof}

3340	\begin{lemma}
3341	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ on locally-trivial pairs (D4$'$)]
3342	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}
3343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIsoOfLocallyTrivial}
3344	  % NOTE: \lean{} pin target absent from Lean source as of iter-271 (verified by grep).
3345	  % This is the LIVE D4' build target (the committed loc-triv route) — a forward reference
3346	  % to a not-yet-formalized declaration, NOT a stale rename. Once the prover lands the decl
3347	  % under this exact name, sync_leanok resolves the pin automatically.
3348	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial, lem:pullback_tensor_map, lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural, lem:pullback_tensor_iso_unit, lem:pullback_tensor_map_basechange, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
3349	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
3350	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{locally trivial}
3351	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then the sheaf-level comparison map
3352	  \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} = \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) of
3353	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map} is an isomorphism:
3354	  \[
3355	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
3356	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3357	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
3358	  \]
3359	  natural in \(M\) and \(N\). This is the comparison the relative Picard consumer needs;
3360	  it is established by promoting the already-built map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) to an
3361	  isomorphism through a chart-chase, with no comparison for arbitrary modules and no
3362	  filtered-colimit machinery.
3363	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
3364	  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback commutes with the tensor product,
3365	  % functorially) and Lemma \ref{lemma-pullback-locally-free} (the pullback of a locally
3366	  % free module is locally free --- the geometric content licensing the chart-chase)
3367	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400 and L2112-2118).
3368	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3369	  % "\begin{lemma}
3370	  % \label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
3371	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
3372	  % a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
3373	  % be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
3374	  % $f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
3375	  % = f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
3376	  % functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
3377	  % \end{lemma}"
3378	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3379	  % "\begin{lemma}
3380	  % \label{lemma-pullback-locally-free}
3381	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
3382	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces. If $\mathcal{G}$ is
3383	  % a locally free $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module, then
3384	  % $f^*\mathcal{G}$ is a locally free $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
3385	  % \end{lemma}"
3386	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemmas
3387	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} and \texttt{lemma-pullback-locally-free}:
3388	  pullback commutes with \(\otimes\) functorially, and the pullback of a locally free
3389	  (locally trivial) module is again locally free; on locally-trivial pairs the comparison
3390	  is therefore an isomorphism, checkable on a trivialising cover.}
3391	\end{lemma}
3392	
3393	\begin{proof}
3394	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial, lem:pullback_tensor_map, lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural, lem:pullback_tensor_iso_unit, lem:pullback_tensor_map_basechange, lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
3395	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemmas \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} and
3396	  \texttt{lemma-pullback-locally-free}.}
3397	
3398	  By local triviality of \(M\) and \(N\) (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}) choose a common
3399	  affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both restrict to the trivial bundle,
3400	  \(M|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and \(N|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) --- the
3401	  common refinement of the two trivialising covers, exactly as in the proof that the
3402	  tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial
3403	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). The preimages
3404	  \(\{f^{-1}U_i\}\) form an open cover of \(Y\). It suffices, by the
3405	  restriction-detects-isomorphism criterion \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}, to show
3406	  that the canonical map \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(M,N)\) restricts to an isomorphism on
3407	  each \(f^{-1}U_i\); the canonical global map being shown locally-iso is exactly what
3408	  lets us conclude (a non-canonical patchwork of local isomorphisms would not glue).
3409	
3410	  Fix \(i\) and write \(g_i : f^{-1}U_i \to U_i\) for the restriction of \(f\), with open
3411	  immersions \(j_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) and \(j_i' : f^{-1}U_i \hookrightarrow Y\),
3412	  so \(f \circ j_i' = j_i \circ g_i\). The base-change coherence
3413	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_basechange} identifies the restriction
3414	  \((j_i')^*\delta^{f}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(M,N)\) with the comparison
3415	  \(\delta^{g_i}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(j_i^*M, j_i^*N)\) for \(g_i\) on the restricted modules,
3416	  and reduces the claim to: \(\delta^{g_i}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(j_i^*M, j_i^*N)\) is an
3417	  isomorphism. On \(U_i\) the restricted modules are trivial, \(j_i^*M \cong
3418	  \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and \(j_i^*N \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\); by naturality of
3419	  \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\) in both arguments (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural})
3420	  these isomorphisms transport \(\delta^{g_i}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(j_i^*M, j_i^*N)\) to the
3421	  comparison on the unit pair \(\delta^{g_i}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(\mathcal{O}_{U_i},
3422	  \mathcal{O}_{U_i})\). That comparison is an isomorphism by
3423	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_unit} --- the unit-pair case, where the structure-sheaf
3424	  comparison \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\) (an isomorphism for all \(g_i\)) and the unitors
3425	  do the work. Hence \(\delta^{g_i}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(j_i^*M, j_i^*N)\) is an isomorphism,
3426	  so is its base-change identification \((j_i')^*\delta^{f}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(M,N)\), and
3427	  thus \(\delta^{f}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(M,N)\) restricts to an isomorphism on each
3428	  \(f^{-1}U_i\). (Both sides are indeed locally trivial on this cover --- \(f^*M, f^*N,
3429	  f^*(M\otimes_X N)\) are locally trivial by \cref{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback} and
3430	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- which is what makes the chart-local
3431	  comparison a map between copies of the structure sheaf.) By
3432	  \cref{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}, \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(M,N)\) is a global
3433	  isomorphism, natural in \(M, N\) by \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural}.
3434	\end{proof}

3436	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
3437	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
3438	
3439	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
3440	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
3441	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
3442	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
3443	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
3444	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
3445	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
3446	
3447	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
3448	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
3449	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
3450	\[
3451	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
3452	  \;:\equiv\;
3453	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
3454	\]
3455	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
3456	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
3457	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
3458	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
3459	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
3460	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
3461	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
3462	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
3463	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
3464

3632	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is invertible the module \(\mathcal{N}\) with
3633	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) is

3637	  \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) and \(M \otimes_X N' \cong \mathcal{O}_X\),

3643	\begin{proof}
3644	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
3645	  This is the standard ``inverse-of-inverse'' argument in a commutative monoid, run
3646	  with isomorphisms in place of equalities. Chain the unitor
3647	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the two hypotheses, the associator
3648	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the braiding
3649	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:
3650	  \[
3651	    N
3652	    \;\cong\; N \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X
3653	    \;\cong\; N \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N')
3654	    \;\cong\; (N \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N'
3655	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X N'
3656	    \;\cong\; N',

3658	  where the second step substitutes \(\mathcal{O}_X \cong M \otimes_X N'\), the
3659	  third is the associator, the fourth uses the braiding
3660	  \(N \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and the outer two are
3661	  the unitors. Hence \(N \cong N'\). For well-definedness on classes, note that if
3662	  \(M \cong M'\) then a tensor inverse of \(M\) is also a tensor inverse of \(M'\)

3713	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
3714	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
3715	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
3716	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
3717	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
3718	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
3719	  \[

3721	  \]

3723	  abelian-group structure with addition

3725	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),

3731	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
3732	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
3733	  map
3734	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
3735	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),

3738	
3739	\begin{proof}
3740	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined, lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
3741	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
3742	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
3743	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
3744	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
3745	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
3746	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
3747	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
3748	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
3749	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
3750	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
3751	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
3752	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
3753	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
3754	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
3755	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
3756	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
3757	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
3758	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
3759	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
3760	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
3761	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
3762	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
3763	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
3764	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
3765	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
3766	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
3767	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
3768	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
3769	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
3770	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
3771	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
3772	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
3773	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
3774	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
3775	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
3776	
3777	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
3778	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
3779	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
3780	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
3781	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
3782	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
3783	
3784	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
3785	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
3786	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
3787	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
3788	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
3789	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
3790	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
3791	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
3792	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
3793	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
3794	\end{proof}

3942	\section{Sheaf internal-hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (the \(\otimes\)-inverse
3943	  infrastructure)}
3944	\label{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}
3945	
3946	The PRIMARY group law (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) needs a
3947	\(\otimes\)-inverse for every line bundle, supplied by
3948	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} as the dual
3949	\(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The standard route
3950	constructs that dual as a special case of the \emph{sheaf internal hom} of
3951	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; over a fixed ring the internal hom of \(R\)-modules is the
3952	linear-coyoneda module \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\), with dual
3953	\(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,\mathbf{1} = M^\vee\), but for the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
3954	no such
3955	internal-hom / monoidal-closed structure exists at the
3956	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\), \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), or general-categorical
3957	level. This section decomposes the construction of the missing object into
3958	individually-formalizable sub-steps, following the slice presentation of the internal
3959	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
3960	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
3961	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
3962	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
3963	
3964	\emph{Status: deferred, off the group's critical path.} The blocks of this section
3965	--- the dual object \(\mathtt{dual}\) and its restriction compatibility
3966	\cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}, the local-triviality of the dual
3967	\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, the gluing engine
3968	\texttt{sheafofmodules\_hom\_of\_local\_compat}, and the discharge remark
3969	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse} --- together constitute the \emph{deferred}
3970	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftrightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) bridge: on a
3971	scheme an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is locally free of rank one, and
3972	conversely (Stacks tag 0B8M), so the two carriers agree, and the locally-trivial
3973	carrier's missing inverse (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}) is recovered
3974	precisely from the dual built here.
