3986	However, the relative Picard \emph{group law} is now carried on the
3987	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
3988	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}, \texttt{pic\_commgroup}), where the inverse is
3989	the membership witness and \emph{no} dual object is constructed. This section is
3990	therefore needed only by a downstream consumer that specifically requires the
3991	locally-trivial form of an inverse --- not by the group law. The blocks are retained
3992	intact for that future use.
3993	
3994	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
3995	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
3996	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
3997	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
3998	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
3999	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
4000	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
4001	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
4002	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
4003	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
4004	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.

4009	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
4010	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
4011	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
4012	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
4013	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom

4017	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
4018	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
4019	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
4020	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
4021	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
4022	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
4023	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
4024	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
4025	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
4026	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
4027	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
4028	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
4029	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
4030	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
4031	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
4032	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
4033	  \[
4034	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
4035	  \]
4036	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
4037	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
4038	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
4039	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
4040	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
4041	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
4042	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
4043	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
4044	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
4045	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
4046	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
4047	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
4048	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
4049	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
4050	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
4051	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
4052	\end{definition}

4110	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
4111	  (tag area 01CM).}
4112	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
4113	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
4114	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
4115	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
4116	  \[
4117	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
4118	      \;:=\;
4119	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
4120	  \]
4121	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
4122	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
4123	  This value module is the slice specialisation
4124	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
4125	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
4126	
4127	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
4128	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
4129	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
4130	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
4131	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
4132	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
4133	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
4134	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
4135	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
4136	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
4137	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
4138	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
4139	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
4140	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
4141	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
4142	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
4143	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
4144	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
4145	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
4146	
4147	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
4148	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
4149	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
4150	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
4151	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
4152	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
4153	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
4154	\end{definition}

4158	\leanok
4159	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
4160	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
4161	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
4162	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
4163	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
4164	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
4165	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
4166	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4167	  % "Consider the rule
4168	  % $$
4169	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
4170	  % $$
4171	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
4172	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
4173	  % abelian groups."
4174	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
4175	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
4176	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
4177	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
4178	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
4179	  is a restriction map
4180	  \[
4181	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
4182	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
4183	  \]
4184	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
4185	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
4186	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
4187	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
4188	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
4189	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
4190	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
4191	  \[
4192	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
4193	  \]
4194	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
4195	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
4196	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
4197	\end{lemma}
4198	
4199	\begin{proof}
4200	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
4201	  \leanok
4202	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
4203	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
4204	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
4205	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
4206	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
4207	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
4208	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
4209	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
4210	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
4211	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
4212	\end{proof}

4214	\begin{definition}
4215	
4216	\leanok
4217	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
4218	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
4219	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
4220	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
4221	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
4222	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
4223	  presheaf,
4224	  \[
4225	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
4226	  \]
4227	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
4228	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
4229	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
4230	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
4231	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
4232	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
4233	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
4234	\end{definition}

4236	\begin{lemma}
4237	
4238	\leanok
4239	[Evaluation/contraction of the internal hom]
4240	  \label{lem:internal_hom_eval}
4241	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval}
4242	  % NOTE (iter-224): \texttt{PresheafOfModules.internalHomEval} is CLOSED axiom-clean
4243	  % (\(\{\texttt{propext}, \texttt{Classical.choice}, \texttt{Quot.sound}\}\)). The full
4244	  % morphism assembles the per-open contraction \texttt{internalHomEvalApp}
4245	  % (the \(R(U)\)-bilinear \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\),
4246	  % \(s \otimes \varphi \mapsto \varphi(s)\)) sectionwise; naturality follows from the
4247	  % evaluate/restrict-commutation argument in the proof body. The iter-222/223 ``whnf
4248	  % heartbeat-bomb'' obstacle was a stale Mathlib-version artifact and is gone.
4249	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
4250	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
4251	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
4252	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3517--L3524).
4253	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4254	  % "There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
4255	  % $$
4256	  % \mathcal{F}
4257	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4258	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
4259	  % \longrightarrow
4260	  % \mathcal{G}.
4261	  % $$"
4262	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
4263	  (tag area 01CM).}
4264	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
4265	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
4266	  \[
4267	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
4268	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
4269	  \]
4270	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
4271	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
4272	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
4273	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
4274	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
4275	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
4276	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
4277	\end{lemma}
4278	
4279	\begin{proof}
4280	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
4281	  \leanok
4282	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
4283	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
4284	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
4285	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
4286	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
4287	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
4288	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
4289	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
4290	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules.
4291	  Concretely, the morphism is defined by specifying, on each open \(U\), the per-open
4292	  contraction \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\).
4293	  The single remaining datum is the naturality square, which reduces to the
4294	  evaluate/restrict-commutation identity \(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\) above: the
4295	  restriction \(\phi|_V\) of a functional inside the dual \(M^\vee\) is its restriction
4296	  to the open \(V\), and restriction is functorial, so evaluating the restricted
4297	  functional on the restricted section agrees with restricting the value of the
4298	  evaluation. This is the same restriction-functoriality argument already used to make
4299	  the internal-hom restriction maps functorial in
4300	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}. This is the counit of the tensor--hom
4301	  adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} specialised to the unit presheaf.
4302	\end{proof}

4304	\begin{lemma}
4305	
4306	\leanok
4307	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
4308	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
4309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
4310	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
4311	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
4312	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
4313	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
4314	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4315	  % "Consider the rule
4316	  % $$
4317	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
4318	  % $$
4319	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
4320	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
4321	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
4322	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
4323	  (tag area 01CM).}
4324	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
4325	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
4326	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
4327	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
4328	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
4329	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
4330	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
4331	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
4332	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
4333	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
4334	  \[
4335	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
4336	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
4337	  \]
4338	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
4339	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
4340	\end{lemma}
4341	
4342	\begin{proof}
4343	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
4344	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
4345	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
4346	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
4347	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
4348	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
4349	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
4350	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
4351	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
4352	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
4353	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
4354	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
4355	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
4356	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
4357	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
4358	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
4359	\end{proof}
4360	
4361	\begin{lemma}
4362	
4363	\leanok
4364	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with the linear dual (H2$'$)]
4365	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
4366	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}
4367	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
4368	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M\) be an
4369	  \(S\)-module. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the formation of the
4370	  linear dual: the canonical map
4371	  \[
4372	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \to_S S\bigr)
4373	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4374	    \bigl(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \to_R R\bigr),
4375	    \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; e^{-1} \circ \varphi,
4376	  \]
4377	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence, with inverse \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\). On both
4378	  sides the \(R\)-module structure is the \(S\)-module structure of \(M\) pulled back
4379	  along \(e\), so that \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\): the left-hand side is the
4380	  \(S\)-linear dual \((M \to_S S)\) regarded as an \(R\)-module by restriction, and the
4381	  right-hand side is the \(R\)-linear dual of the restricted module
4382	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M\) into the restricted ground ring. This is the dual
4383	  analogue of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: it is to the internal hom
4384	  / dual exactly what that lemma is to the tensor product. It is the \emph{H2$'$
4385	  ingredient} of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism of the C-bridge
4386	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} --- the ring-iso/dual commutation that, composed with
4387	  the open-immersion pushforward comparison H1, identifies the dual of a restriction
4388	  with the restriction of the dual.
4389	\end{lemma}
4390	
4391	\begin{proof}
4392	
4393	\leanok
4394	  The two assignments \(\varphi \mapsto e^{-1} \circ \varphi\) and
4395	  \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\) are mutually inverse, since \(e \circ e^{-1} =
4396	  \mathrm{id}_S\) and \(e^{-1} \circ e = \mathrm{id}_R\) as ring homomorphisms, so the
4397	  two composites are the identity on functionals. Each assignment is \(R\)-linear:
4398	  additivity is immediate, and for a scalar \(r \in R\) the defining relation
4399	  \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\) of the pulled-back module structure makes each linearity
4400	  check a single rewrite --- \(e^{-1}\) (resp.\ \(e\)) intertwines the \(R\)-action on
4401	  the source with the \(R\)-action on the target precisely because \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\)
4402	  are inverse ring homomorphisms identifying \(R\) with \(S\). The forward map is
4403	  therefore an \(R\)-linear bijection, i.e.\ an \(R\)-linear equivalence; packaging the
4404	  two one-sided maps with the inverse identities gives
4405	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}\).
4406	\end{proof}

4408	\begin{definition}
4409	
4410	\leanok
4411	[Precomposition equivalence on dual sections]
4412	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
4413	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualPrecompEquiv}
4414	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual}
4415	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings and let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\)
4416	  be an isomorphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. On a fixed open \(U\), precomposition
4417	  with \(e\) gives an \(R(U)\)-linear equivalence between the dual sections
4418	  \[
4419	    \mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U \;\colon\;
4420	      \bigl(M'|_U \to R|_U\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \bigl(M|_U \to R|_U\bigr),
4421	      \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; \varphi \circ e|_U,
4422	  \]
4423	  with inverse \(\psi \mapsto \psi \circ (e^{-1})|_U\). This is the section-level core of
4424	  the contravariant functoriality of the presheaf dual \(M \mapsto M^\vee\)
4425	  (\cref{def:presheaf_dual}) in isomorphisms: precomposing a functional on \(M'\) with
4426	  the comparison isomorphism produces a functional on \(M\), \(R(U)\)-linearly and
4427	  invertibly.
4428	\end{definition}

4430	\begin{definition}
4431	
4432	\leanok
4433	[The presheaf dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(M'^\vee \cong M^\vee\)]
4434	  \label{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
4435	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso}
4436	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
4437	  An isomorphism \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules induces a
4438	  canonical isomorphism of presheaf duals
4439	  \[
4440	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; M'^\vee \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M^\vee
4441	  \]
4442	  --- note the reversal of direction, the dual being contravariant --- assembled
4443	  open-by-open from the precomposition equivalences
4444	  \(\mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U\) of \cref{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}.
4445	  Naturality in \(U\), i.e.\ compatibility of these sectionwise equivalences with
4446	  restriction, is the routine check that precomposition by \(e\) commutes with
4447	  restricting functionals.
4448	\end{definition}

4450	\begin{definition}
4451	
4452	\leanok
4453	[The sheaf-level dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(\mathtt{dual}\,M' \cong \mathtt{dual}\,M\)]
4454	  \label{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}
4455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso}
4456	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
4457	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an isomorphism in
4458	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of sheaf-level
4459	  duals
4460	  \[
4461	    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; \mathtt{dual}\,M' \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4462	      \mathtt{dual}\,M,
4463	  \]
4464	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf isomorphism \(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e\) of
4465	  \cref{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso} through the sheaf-level dual of
4466	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}. It is the dual (contravariant) analogue of the
4467	  functoriality of the tensor product in isomorphisms: where an isomorphism of a tensor

4469	  induces an isomorphism of duals in the reverse direction.

4472	\begin{lemma}
4473	
4474	[The per-section dual transport \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\)]
4475	  \label{lem:slice_dual_transport}
4476	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sliceDualTransport}
4477	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso, def:dual_unit_iso_gen, lem:image_preimage_of_le, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom, lem:slice_dual_transport_inv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_comp_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv_comp}
4478	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes and let
4479	  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Write \(\alpha\) for the open-immersion
4480	  structure-presheaf natural transformation with components
4481	  \(\alpha_U = (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,U).\mathrm{inv}\), a \emph{\(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)}-level
4482	  ring isomorphism, and \(\beta = \mathtt{whiskerRight}\,\alpha\,
4483	  (\mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\) for its
4484	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\)-level shadow, with component
4485	  \(\beta_V \colon \mathcal{O}_Y(V) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)
4486	  (\(fV := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(V)\)). Then for every open \(V \subseteq Y\) there is an
4487	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
4488	  \[
4489	    \bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V)
4490	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4491	    \bigl(\mathtt{dual}\,((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V),
4492	  \]
4493	  realised as a single \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\) that packages \emph{both} leg (A)
4494	  --- the slice-Hom base-change reindexing across \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) --- and leg (B)
4495	  --- the unit codomain ring-iso swap. The naturality of the section family in \(W\) has
4496	  \emph{two} distinct parts: the underlying base-morphism uniqueness in
4497	  \((\mathtt{Over}\,V.\mathrm{unop})^{\mathrm{op}}\) is \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (a thin
4498	  poset has at most one inclusion between objects), but the accompanying equation of
4499	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module maps is a genuine, separate obligation: it is the
4500	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-ring iso
4501	  \(\beta_W\), which \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) does \emph{not} discharge.
4502	\end{lemma}
4503	
4504	\begin{proof}
4505	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso, def:dual_unit_iso_gen, lem:image_preimage_of_le, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom, lem:slice_dual_transport_inv, def:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_comp_inv, lem:dual_unit_ring_swap_inv_comp}
4506	  The isomorphism is produced from an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear equivalence between the
4507	  left-hand module --- the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module inherited from
4508	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) --- and the right-hand \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\),
4509	  assembled via \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\), with both module structures
4510	  identified explicitly.
4511	
4512	  \emph{Leg (A): the slice-Hom reindexing, built categorically.} A dual section
4513	  \(\varphi\) of the left-hand value is a morphism \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M.\mathtt{val} \to
4514	  \mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,\mathbf{1}_X\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\). Its image is the dual
4515	  section over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) whose component at \(W \le V\) (with image
4516	  \(fW := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W)\)) is the categorical image
4517	  \[

4611	      operations are expressed pointwise on the underlying morphisms and the scalars matched by

4617	      \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}.

4621	  different character, and it is a mistake to discharge the whole square by

4634	  \varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W)\). This \(\varepsilon\)-naturality step is a
4635	  genuine, separate obligation; the thin-poset argument of (a) does \emph{not} discharge it.

4645	  prover invokes that naturality field directly; the thin-poset \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\)

4829	\begin{proof}
4830	  \uses{def:dual_unit_iso_gen}
4831	  Proved directly in Lean --- a direct instance of \cref{def:dual_unit_iso_gen}.
4832	\end{proof}

4834	\begin{lemma}[Reverse slice dual transport (the \(\mathtt{invFun}\) of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport})]
4835	  \label{lem:slice_dual_transport_inv}
4836	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sliceDualTransportInv}
4837	  \uses{def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom, lem:image_preimage_of_le, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}
4838	  \textit{Source: internal categorical construction; no external reference.}
4839	  In the setting of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}, with \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) an open
4840	  immersion, \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), \(V \subseteq Y\) an open and \(\beta\)
4841	  the open-immersion structure-ring morphism, there is a reverse transport: a dual section
4842	  \(\psi : \mathtt{restr}_V\,((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta)\,M.\mathtt{val}) \to
4843	  \mathtt{restr}_V\,\mathbf 1_Y\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) is sent to an \(X\)-slice dual
4844	  section \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M.\mathtt{val} \to \mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,\mathbf 1_X\) over
4845	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\), where \(fV = f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(V)\). This is the inverse leg
4846	  of the forward equivalence \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.
4847	\end{lemma}
4848	\begin{proof}
4849	  \uses{def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom, lem:image_preimage_of_le, lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}
4850	  We build the underlying \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom}\) component-by-component over the
4851	  \(X\)-slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\), mirroring the forward component formula of
4852	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} with two changes.
4853	
4854	  \emph{The component at \(W''\).} Fix \(W'' \le fV\) and set \(W' := W''.\mathrm{left}\) and
4855	  \(P := f^{-1}(W')\) for its preimage. Because \(f\) is an open immersion and
4856	  \(fV \subseteq \mathrm{range}\,f\), the open \(W' \le fV\) lies in the image of
4857	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\), so the down-set identity
4858	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(P) = W'\) holds --- but only \emph{propositionally}
4859	  (\cref{lem:image_preimage_of_le}); this is the single set-theoretic fact the inverse
4860	  reindexing depends on. The component of the reverse section at \(W''\) is the \(X\)-slice
4861	  mirror of the forward component:
4862	  \[
4863	    M.\mathtt{val}(\mathtt{eqToHom})
4864	      \;\circ\;
4865	    (\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,P).\mathrm{hom}).\mathrm{map}
4866	      \bigl(\psi_{(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(P \le V))}\bigr)
4867	      \;\circ\;
4868	    \mathtt{dualUnitRingSwapHom}\,f\,P,
4869	  \]
4870	  conjugated on source and target by the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) transports arising from the
4871	  propositional equality \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(P) = W'\) (the mirror of
4872	  \(\mathtt{homLocalSection}\)). Compared with the forward formula, this map uses
4873	  \((f.\mathtt{appIso}\,P).\mathrm{hom}\) in place of its inverse --- because the reverse
4874	  reindex turns a \(\mathcal{O}_Y(P)\)-section map into a \(\mathcal{O}_X(fP)\)-map --- and
4875	  its codomain swap is \(\mathtt{dualUnitRingSwapHom}\) (\cref{def:dual_unit_ring_swap_hom}),
4876	  the inverse of \(\varepsilon\) in the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction
4877	  (\cref{lem:isiso_eps_restrictscalars_appiso_hom}).
4878	
4879	  \emph{Additivity and \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity} follow exactly as in the forward leg
4880	  (steps (i)--(iii) of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}): identify the two module
4881	  presentations, match the X- and Y-side scalars by the \(\beta\)-naturality ring identity,
4882	  and apply the change-of-rings compatibility, the module-level shadow of
4883	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}.
4884	
4885	  \emph{Naturality} of the component family across morphisms of \((\mathtt{Over}\,(fV))^{\mathrm{op}}\)
4886	  reduces, after the thin-poset \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) on the indexing morphisms, to
4887	  the \(\varepsilon\)-naturality of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-ring iso,
4888	  exactly as in the forward leg. The unit handling is the presheaf dual-of-unit
4889	  identification \cref{def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}. The whole family is reindexed by the
4890	  inverse of the down-set bijection \(W'' \leftrightarrow f^{-1}(W'')\) supplied by
4891	  \cref{lem:image_preimage_of_le}.
4892	\end{proof}

4894	\begin{lemma}
4895	
4896	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
4897	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
4898	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
4899	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:slice_dual_transport}
4900	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
4901	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
4902	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
4903	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4904	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
4905	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
4906	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
4907	  % then the canonical map
4908	  % $$
4909	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
4910	  % \longrightarrow
4911	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
4912	  % $$
4913	  % is an isomorphism."
4914	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
4915	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
4916	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
4917	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
4918	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
4919	  \[
4920	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
4921	  \]
4922	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
4923	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
4924	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
4925	\end{lemma}
4926	
4927	\begin{proof}
4928	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv, lem:slice_dual_transport}
4929	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
4930	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
4931	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
4932	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
4933	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
4934	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
4935	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
4936	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
4937	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
4938	
4939	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
4940	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
4941	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
4942	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
4943	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
4944	  \[
4945	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
4946	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
4947	  \]
4948	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
4949	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
4950	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
4951	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
4952	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
4953	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
4954	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
4955	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
4956	
4957	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
4958	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
4959	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
4960	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
4961	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
4962	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
4963	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
4964	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
4965	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
4966	  \emph{exactly}
4967	  \[
4968	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
4969	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4970	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
4971	  \]
4972	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
4973	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
4974	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
4975	  two-leg split of the whole step.
4976	
4977	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
4978	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
4979	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
4980	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
4981	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
4982	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
4983	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
4984	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
4985	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
4986	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
4987	
4988	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
4989	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
4990	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
4991	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
4992	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
4993	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
4994	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
4995	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
4996	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
4997	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\texttt{restrictscalars\_ringiso\_strongmonoidal}).
4998	
4999	  \emph{The combined leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Both legs are
5000	  realised together by a single \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
5001	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), built
5002	  \emph{by hand} --- it is \emph{not} obtained by consuming a sheaf-level slice equivalence.
5003	  This is essential: the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of modules} over an open immersion
5004	  compares the restriction functors and the units, but carries \emph{no} internal-hom or
5005	  dual content; the assertion realized by leg (A) --- that the dual commutes with slice
5006	  reindexing along the open-immersion direct-image functor \(f_{!}\) on opens
5007	  (\(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\)) --- cannot be supplied by such an object without a
5008	  monoidal-closed structure on sheaves of modules, which this development deliberately does
5009	  \emph{not} carry (cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The atom must
5010	  therefore be constructed directly.
5011	
5012	  Concretely (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), the forward component of
5013	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) at \(W \le V\) is the categorical image
5014	  \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,(\varphi_{\bullet})\) of the dual-section
5015	  component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
5016	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), post-composed with
5017	  the leg-(B) codomain swap \(\mathtt{inv}(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W))\).
5018	  Leg (A) is built categorically via \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\):
5019	  a genuine change-of-rings functor acts here, so the reindexing must be applied as a
5020	  functor map rather than pointwise. Leg (B)'s unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) is invertible
5021	  because \(\beta_W\) is a ring isomorphism, as detailed in \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.
5022	  Because \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset, the
5023	  naturality squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The forward and inverse maps
5024	  identify the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom value over
5025	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearly.
5026	
5027	  The Step-4 residual is then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\) applied \emph{directly} to
5028	  the family \(V \mapsto \mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) --- which already packages
5029	  \emph{both} legs (A) and (B) --- with the outer naturality square thin-poset-trivial. No
5030	  separate leg-(B) step is interposed in \(\mathtt{isoMk}\): the per-section atom of
5031	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} carries the full leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) content. The outer
5032	  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) naturality square follows from the per-section family naturality
5033	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport.naturality}\) of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}: the
5034	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality is established first at the level of individual sections, and the
5035	  outer presheaf-morphism naturality of \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) then follows.
5036	
5037	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
5038	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
5039	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
5040	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
5041	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
5042	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5043	
5044	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5045	  (\texttt{presheaf\_pushforward\_adj\_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5046	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5047	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5048	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5049	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5050	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5051	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5052	
5053	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5054	  \texttt{open\_immersion\_slice\_sheaf\_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5055	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5056	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5057	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5058	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5059	
5060	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5061	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5062	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5063	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
5064	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
5065	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5066	
5067	  \emph{The inverse-uniqueness shortcut is not viable.} One might hope to derive the
5068	  isomorphism from the already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of
5069	  monoidal inverses --- the dual being the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and
5070	  restriction commuting with \(\otimes\) --- sidestepping leg (A). The abstract statements of
5071	  this idiom (\(\mathtt{hasRightDualOfEquivalence}\), \(\mathtt{rightDualIso}\)) require,
5072	  however, four structures the present setting lacks: a registered \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\)
5073	  instance on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) (the monoidal structure here is carried entirely
5074	  by hand), a \emph{strong} (not merely lax/oplax) monoidal restriction functor, restriction
5075	  being an \emph{equivalence} (false for a non-surjective open immersion, which is a
5076	  localization), and an exact pairing \(\mathtt{ExactPairing}\,M\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M)\) (which
5077	  exists only for dualizable \(M\), whereas the lemma is stated for general \(M\)). Even
5078	  restricted to the invertible consumer this would require building a coevaluation and
5079	  verifying the zig-zag identities --- strictly \emph{more} infrastructure than leg (A) --- so
5080	  the construction commits to legs (A) and (B) above.
5081	\end{proof}

5083	\begin{lemma}[Dual of the structure sheaf]
5084	  \label{lem:dual_unit_iso}
5085	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_unit_iso}
5086	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
5087	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
5088	  structure sheaf itself,
5089	  \[
5090	    \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
5091	  \]
5092	  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf-level ``evaluation at \(1\)'' isomorphism
5093	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\): every endomorphism of the
5094	  unit presheaf is multiplication by its value at the global section \(1\), so evaluation
5095	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
5096	\end{lemma}
5097	
5098	\begin{proof}
5099	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}
5100	  At the presheaf level the dual of the unit \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the internal
5101	  hom \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1})\), whose value on an open \(U\) is the module
5102	  of \(\mathcal{O}(U)\)-linear endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{1}|_U\). Each such endomorphism is
5103	  determined by its value at the global section \(1\) and equals multiplication by that
5104	  value; hence \emph{evaluation at \(1\)} is itself the sectionwise isomorphism
5105	  \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}) \cong \mathbf{1}\) of presheaves of modules, with
5106	  inverse the scalar-action map \(r \mapsto (\,\cdot\,r)\). (No left unitor is needed: the
5107	  identification is the direct evaluation-at-\(1\) / global-scalar-multiplication
5108	  isomorphism, not the composite through \(\mathbf{1} \otimes \mathbf{1} \cong \mathbf{1}\).)
5109	  Sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} yields
5110	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_Y \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\). The scheme-level alias of this
5111	  presheaf evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism is
5112	  \(\mathtt{presheafDualUnitIso}\) (\cref{def:presheaf_dual_unit_iso}).
5113	\end{proof}
5114	
5115	\begin{lemma}
5116	
5117	[The dual of a line bundle is a line bundle]
5118	  \label{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_isLocallyTrivial}
5120	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso, lem:dual_unit_iso, def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5121	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
5122	  % modules), Lemma~lemma-constructions-invertible (item~2)
5123	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200--L4213).
5124	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
5125	  % "\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
5126	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
5127	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
5128	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
5129	  % is an isomorphism."
5130	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR,
5131	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (item~2).}
5132	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
5133	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
5134	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of
5135	  rank one, i.e.\ \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,L)\).
5136	\end{lemma}
5137	
5138	\begin{proof}
5139	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:dual_restrict_iso, lem:dual_unit_iso, def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5140	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
5141	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
5142	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
5143	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
5144	  \[
5145	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
5146	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
5147	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
5148	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
5149	  \]
5150	  \begin{enumerate}
5151	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
5152	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
5153	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
5154	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
5155	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
5156	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
5157	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level

5172	\begin{lemma}
5173	
5174	\leanok
5175	[Local isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules glue to a global isomorphism]
5176	  \label{lem:isiso_of_isiso_restrict}
5177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_restrict}
5178	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
5179	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
5180	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
5181	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
5182	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
5183	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
5184	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
5185	  isomorphism.
5186	\end{lemma}
5187	
5188	\begin{proof}
5189	
5190	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
5191	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
5192	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
5193	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
5194	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
5195	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
5196	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
5197	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
5198	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
5199	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
5200	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
5201	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
5202	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
5203	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
5204	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
5205	  itself is an isomorphism.
5206	\end{proof}
